# 1 Overview

## 1.1 Background

The Property & Casualty (P&C) insurance industry has long struggled with underperforming portfolios where the root causes of poor profitability and high volatility remain unknown. Traditional approaches to data-driven decision-making in insurance have largely focused on dashboard reporting, which is limited by the general lack of advanced analytical capabilities which offers a more powerful alternative. By combining data science, risk knowledge and industry expertise, insurers can generate actionable insights to grow, optimise existing portfolios and improve operational efficiency. As noted by the Swiss Re Institute (2019), leveraging visual analytics can potentially help investigate trends in underlying loss drivers, while data enrichment using external data can help refine segmentation and underwriting strategy.

In this study, a motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset from a Spanish insurance company, made publicly available via Mendeley Data, is used to demonstrate the potential of visual analytics techniques in exploring and analysing key factors influencing portfolio profitability and volatility.

## 1.2 The Data

The data for this exercise is the motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset retrieved from [Mendeley Data](https://data.mendeley.com/datasets/sw4jmdb2sm/1).

The dataset contains **354,140 policy records** across **47 variables**, covering the period from 2022 to 2024. Variables are grouped into four broad categories:

| Category | Description |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Policy & Insured characteristics | Policy type, status, business origin, payment frequency, bonus score |
| Driver & Vehicle characteristics | Driver age, vehicle age, fuel type, vehicle brand, vehicle value, area |
| Premiums | Total and coverage-level premiums collected |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | Claims counts, incurred costs, and exposure duration |

## 1.3 The Task

Using appropriate **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** methods and **ggplot2** functions, this study aims to:

-   Apply suitable **univariate** visual analytics techniques to explore the distributions and characteristics of numerical and categorical variables.

-   Apply suitable **bivariate** visual analytics techniques to investigate and statistically validate factors influencing the **profitability** and **volatility** of the insurance portfolio.

# 2 Loading Packages

| Package | Description |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| [**tidyverse**](https://www.tidyverse.org/) | A collection of R packages designed for data science, used for data import, wrangling and transformation |

| [**knitr**](https://yihui.org/knitr/) | For dynamic report generation and displaying formatted tables |

| [**scales**](https://scales.r-lib.org/) | For formatting axis labels and annotations in ggplot2 |
| [**ggridges**](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/ggridges/vignettes/introduction.html) | For creating ridgeline plots to visualise distributions |
| [**ggdist**](https://mjskay.github.io/ggdist/) | For visualising distributions and uncertainty |

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, readxl, knitr,
               patchwork, ggthemes, scales,
               ggridges, ggdist, ggpubr,
               ggstatsplot, naniar, ggiraph)
```
:::


# 3 Importing the Data

In this section, the following steps are taken to import and perform initial checks on the data, from reading in the raw data to verifying its structure. The **tidyverse** package will be used, specifically **readr** for reading in the data and **dplyr** for data manipulation.

The code chunk below imports the motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset using `read_delim()` of the **readr** package. The dataset uses a semicolon (`;`) as its delimiter instead of a comma, so `delim = ";"` is specified.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- read_delim("Dataset of motor insurance portfolio.csv",
                        delim = ";",
                        show_col_types = FALSE)
```
:::


The dataset is in a tibble dataframe, containing **354,140 observations (rows)** across **47 variables (columns)**. Each observation corresponds to a motor vehicle insurance policy record from a Spanish non-life insurance company.

We use `glimpse()` of the **dplyr** package to get a quick overview of the dataset structure, including the number of rows, columns and data types of each variable.

# 4 Data Wrangling

The following flowchart shows the main variables used and created in this study.

## 4.1 Variable Selection

Below are the variables retained for this study, which can be grouped into 4 broad categories of Policy & Insured Characteristics, Driver & Vehicle Characteristics, Premiums, and Exposure, Claims & Incurred. Details are extracted from the cookbook(`Descriptive_of_variables.xlsx`):

| Category | Variable | Description | Type | Encoding |
|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| Policy & Insured | `policy_type` | Main coverage structure | CAT | TP, TPG, CC, COMP_E, COMP_N |
| Policy & Insured | `policy_status` | Policy active or cancelled | CAT | A - Active, C - Cancelled |
| Policy & Insured | `business_type` | Business origin | CAT | NB - New Business, P - Portfolio |
| Policy & Insured | `payment_frequency` | Billing frequency | CAT | A - Annual, S - Semi-annual, Q - Quarterly |
| Policy & Insured | `bonus_score` | Past claims experience | CAT | G - Good, N - Neutral, B - Bad |
| Driver & Vehicle | `driver_age` | Age of main driver | NUM | In years |
| Driver & Vehicle | `vehicle_age` | Age of insured vehicle | NUM | In years |
| Driver & Vehicle | `vehicle_value` | Declared insured value | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Driver & Vehicle | `fuel_type` | Vehicle fuel type | CAT | D - Diesel, G - Gasoline |
| Driver & Vehicle | `vehicle_brand` | Brand of vehicle | CAT | 65 brands |
| Driver & Vehicle | `municipality_type` | Geographic classification | CAT | I - Inland, C - Coastal, IS - Islands |
| Driver & Vehicle | `circulation_area` | Circulation environment | CAT | U - Urban, R - Rural |
| Premiums | `total_premium` | Total premium collected | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `liability_premium` | Liability coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `property_damage_premium` | Property damage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `theft_premium` | Theft coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `fire_premium` | Fire coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `glass_premium` | Glass coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `legal_protection_premium` | Legal protection premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `occupants_premium` | Occupants coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `total_exposure` | Time-on-risk in policy years | NUM | 0 to 1 |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `total_claims` | Total claims reported | NUM | Count |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `total_incurred` | Total incurred cost of claims | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `loss_ratio`\* | Profitability measure | NUM | total_incurred / total_premium |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `claim_frequency`\* | Volatility measure | NUM | total_claims / total_exposure |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `has_claim`\* | Binary claim indicator | NUM | 1 if claims \> 0, else 0 |

\*Derived variables created in Section 4.5.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  select(insured_id, year,
         policy_type, policy_status, business_type,
         payment_frequency, bonus_score,
         driver_age, vehicle_age, age_driving_licence,
         fuel_type, vehicle_value, seats,
         power_to_weight_ratio, vehicle_brand,
         municipality_type, circulation_area,
         total_premium, liability_premium,
         property_damage_premium, theft_premium,
         fire_premium, glass_premium,
         legal_protection_premium, occupants_premium,
         total_exposure, liability_exposure,
         total_claims, total_incurred)
```
:::


The resulting dataset retains **354,140 observations** across **30 variables**.

## 4.3 Recoding Categorical Variables

`recode()` is primarily used to replace specific values in a vector with new ones. The recoded variable is then assigned using `mutate()` of the **dplyr** package.

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_type = recode(policy_type,
    "TP"     = "Third-Party",
    "TPG"    = "Third-Party with Glass",
    "CC"     = "Third-Party Combined",
    "COMP_E" = "Comprehensive with Excess",
    "COMP_N" = "Comprehensive without Excess"))
```

## 4.4 Data Type Conversion

From the `glimpse()` output earlier, the categorical variables are currently stored as character (`chr`) data type. They need to be converted to **factors** (`fct`) so that R treats them correctly in visualisations and statistical analyses. `mutate()` and `as.factor()` of the **dplyr** package are used to perform the conversion.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_type       = as.factor(policy_type),
         policy_status     = as.factor(policy_status),
         business_type     = as.factor(business_type),
         payment_frequency = as.factor(payment_frequency),
```

## 4.5 Variable Creation

Three new variables are created to support the analysis of portfolio profitability and volatility. `mutate()` of the **dplyr** package is used to create these variables.

### 4.5.1 Loss Ratio

`loss_ratio` is calculated as the ratio of total incurred costs to total premium collected. A loss ratio greater than 1 indicates that the insurer paid out more in claims than it collected in premiums, reflecting poor profitability.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(loss_ratio = total_incurred / total_premium)
```
:::


### 4.5.2 Claim Frequency

`claim_frequency` is calculated as the ratio of total claims to total exposure. It measures how often claims occur per policy year, reflecting the volatility of the portfolio.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(claim_frequency = total_claims / total_exposure)
```
:::


### 4.5.3 Has Claim

`has_claim` is a binary indicator variable that takes the value 1 if a policy has at least one claim, and 0 otherwise. It is useful for identifying which policies have experienced a loss.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(has_claim = ifelse(total_claims > 0, 1, 0))
```

# 5 Univariate Analysis

## 5.1 Target Variables

The target variables are `loss_ratio`, `claim_frequency`, `total_incurred` and `has_claim`, which measure portfolio profitability, volatility, claim severity and claim occurrence respectively. As some variables contain extreme outliers, the analysis is restricted to a reasonable range for visualisation purposes. Before diving into the Exploratory Data Analysis, the distributions of key variables are examined individually to better understand their characteristics and identify any notable patterns.

::: panel-tabset
## Loss Ratio

`loss_ratio` measures the ratio of total incurred costs to total premium collected. A loss ratio greater than 1 indicates that the insurer paid out more in claims than it collected in premiums.

A raincloud plot is used here as it combines a half-violin density plot, boxplot and individual data points into a single view, providing a more complete picture of the distribution than a histogram or boxplot alone — simultaneously showing shape, central tendency, spread and outliers.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance_lr <- insurance %>%
  filter(loss_ratio < 10,
         loss_ratio > 0)

ggplot(insurance_lr,
       aes(x = 0, y = loss_ratio)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA,
               fill = "#4a7c6f") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2,
               outlier.shape = NA,
               fill = "#7aadab",
               alpha = 0.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Loss Ratio",
       subtitle = "Policies with loss ratio between 0 and 10",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Loss Ratio") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank())
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The raincloud plot reveals that `loss_ratio` is highly right-skewed among policies with claims. Most policies record a loss ratio between 1.0 and 2.5, indicating that incurred costs typically exceed premiums collected. The long right tail suggests that a small number of policies experience exceptionally high losses, which significantly impacts overall portfolio profitability.

A histogram paired with a boxplot is used below to complement the raincloud plot — the histogram reveals the precise frequency distribution while the boxplot highlights the median and interquartile range in a compact format.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
p1 <- ggplot(insurance_lr,
             aes(x = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.1,
                 fill = "#4a7c6f",
                 colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean(loss_ratio)),
             colour = "red",
             linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(loss_ratio)),
             colour = "blue",
             linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = mean(insurance_lr$loss_ratio) + 0.3,
           y = Inf, vjust = 2,
           label = paste("Mean:", round(mean(insurance_lr$loss_ratio), 2)),
           colour = "red", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = median(insurance_lr$loss_ratio) + 0.3,
           y = Inf, vjust = 4,
           label = paste("Median:", round(median(insurance_lr$loss_ratio), 2)),
           colour = "blue", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Loss Ratio",
       x = "Loss Ratio",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(insurance_lr,
             aes(x = loss_ratio,
                 y = "")) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#7aadab",
               outlier.alpha = 0.3,
               outlier.size = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "Loss Ratio",
       y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p2 / p1 +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1, 3.5))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-29-1.png){width=672}
:::

The distribution of `loss_ratio` is highly right-skewed, with the majority of policies having a loss ratio of 0 (no claims made). The mean loss ratio is higher than the median, indicating the presence of a small number of policies with very high loss ratios that pull the mean upward.

## ECDF of Incurred Amount by Policy Type 

An Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function (ECDF) plot is used below to complement the histogram. Unlike a histogram where the shape can change depending on bin width, the ECDF makes no such assumption, it shows directly what proportion of claims fall below any given cost level for each policy type, making it easier to compare tail behaviour across groups at a glance.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance %>%
  filter(total_incurred > 0, total_incurred < 10000) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = total_incurred, colour = policy_type)) +
  stat_ecdf(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Comprehensive with Excess"    = "#4a7c6f",
    "Comprehensive without Excess" = "#7aadab",
    "Third-Party"                  = "#d4e8e1",
    "Third-Party Combined"         = "#2d5a51",
    "Third-Party with Glass"       = "#a8d5c2")) +
  labs(title = "ECDF of Total Incurred by Policy Type",
       subtitle = "Policies with total incurred between €0 and €10,000",
       x = "Total Incurred (€)",
       y = "Cumulative Proportion",
       colour = "Policy Type") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-33-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

The ECDF plot shows that all five policy types follow a broadly similar distribution, with most claims concentrated below €2,500. **Third-Party with Glass** and **Comprehensive without Excess** curves rise more steeply in the lower cost range, indicating a higher proportion of small, cheap claims, consistent with the finding that the absence of an excess encourages filing of minor claims. **Comprehensive with Excess** and **Third-Party Combined** have slightly flatter curves, suggesting a wider spread of claim costs including a larger proportion of mid-to-high cost claims. **Third-Party** shows the most gradual curve, indicating the greatest spread of claim costs across the full range though its small sample size means this should be interpreted with caution.



::: {.cell}

## Has Claim

`has_claim` is a binary variable indicating whether a policy has recorded at least one claim (1) or no claims (0). A bar chart is used here as it is the most appropriate chart type for a binary categorical variable — directly showing the count and proportion of each group without implying any ordering or magnitude between them.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance %>%
  mutate(has_claim = ifelse(has_claim == 1,
                            "Has Claim",
                            "No Claim")) %>%
  count(has_claim) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = has_claim,
             y = n,
             fill = has_claim)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5,
            size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Has Claim" = "#4a7c6f",
                                "No Claim" = "#7aadab")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Claim Occurrence",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Number of Policies") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-35-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The bar chart shows that **85.7%** of policies in the portfolio have not recorded any claims, while only **14.3%** have experienced at least one claim. This is typical of motor insurance portfolios where most policyholders do not make a claim in a given year. Despite being a small proportion, the 14.3% of policies with claims are of particular interest as they are the primary drivers of portfolio profitability and volatility.

The boxplot shows that the middle 50% of drivers fall between approximately 39 and 58 years old, with a small number of older drivers above 75 appearing as outliers.

## Bonus Score

`bonus_score` reflects the policyholder's past claims experience — Good (G), Neutral (N) or Bad (B). A bar chart is used here as it is the most suitable chart type for an ordinal categorical variable with a small number of levels, making proportional differences easy to compare at a glance.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance %>%
  count(bonus_score) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(bonus_score, -n),
             y = n,
             fill = bonus_score)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5,
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Good"    = "#4a7c6f",
                                "Neutral" = "#7aadab",
                                "Bad"     = "#d4e8e1")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Bonus Score",
       x = "Bonus Score",
       y = "Number of Policies") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-37-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The bar chart shows that **96.5%** of policies belong to policyholders with a Good bonus score, indicating a strong claims history across the portfolio. Only **2.2%** are Neutral and **1.3%** are Bad. This suggests that the portfolio is largely made up of low-risk, experienced policyholders who have rarely or never made a claim.
:::

## 5.3 Vehicle Characteristics

This section explores the distribution of vehicle-related variables in the portfolio, specifically `vehicle_age`, `vehicle_value`, `fuel_type` and `vehicle_brand`.

::: panel-tabset
## Vehicle Age

`vehicle_age` represents the age of the insured vehicle in years. A histogram and boxplot combination is used here for the same reasons as `driver_age` — the histogram reveals the shape and spread of the age distribution, while the boxplot highlights central tendency and outliers.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
p9 <- ggplot(insurance,
             aes(x = vehicle_age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1,
                 fill = "#4a7c6f",

             colour = "red",

  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(vehicle_age)),
             colour = "blue",

             linewidth = 0.8) +

           y = Inf, vjust = 2,

           colour = "red", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = median(insurance$vehicle_age) + 5,
           y = Inf, vjust = 4,
           label = paste("Median:", round(median(insurance$vehicle_age), 1)),
           colour = "blue", size = 3) +

p10 <- ggplot(insurance,

p10 / p9 +
```

## Vehicle Brand

`vehicle_brand` represents the brand of the insured vehicle. As there are 65 brands in the dataset, only the top 10 most common brands are shown for clarity. The top 7 brands are all European, with SEAT being the only Spanish brand in the list. Toyota is the only Asian brand to appear in the top 10, ranking last with **4%** of policies.

# 6 Exploratory Data Analysis

In this section, bivariate visual analytics techniques are applied to investigate and statistically validate the factors influencing the profitability and volatility of the motor insurance portfolio.

## 6.1 Year-on-Year Loss Ratio Trend

A line chart is used here as it is the most appropriate chart type for communicating directional trends over time — making it easy to identify whether portfolio profitability is improving or deteriorating across the three-year period. The break-even line at 1.0 provides an immediate visual reference point.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    loss_ratio    = sum(total_incurred) / sum(total_premium),
    claim_freq    = sum(total_claims) / sum(total_exposure)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = loss_ratio), colour = "#4a7c6f", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(y = loss_ratio), colour = "#4a7c6f", size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  geom_text(aes(y = loss_ratio,
                label = round(loss_ratio, 3)),
            vjust = -0.8, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Portfolio Loss Ratio by Year",
       subtitle = "Red dashed line = break-even (loss ratio = 1.0)",
       x = "Year",
       y = "Loss Ratio") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-48-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The line chart reveals how the portfolio loss ratio has evolved over the 2022–2024 period. The portfolio loss ratio has been rising steadily from **0.657 in 2022** to **0.665 in 2023** and **0.747 in 2024**. While the portfolio remains below the break-even line of 1.0, meaning the insurer is still profitable overall the upward trend is a concern. If this trend continues, the portfolio could approach or breach the break-even point in future years, making it important to investigate and address the underlying loss drivers now.

## 6.2 Bubble Chart: Severity, Frequency and Premium Volume

A bubble chart is used here to simultaneously visualise three dimensions — average claim severity, claim frequency and premium volume — across policy types. This provides a more complete picture of risk concentration than any single measure alone, allowing the identification of segments that are simultaneously high risk and low premium.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance %>%
  filter(has_claim == 1) %>%
  group_by(policy_type) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_severity  = mean(total_incurred),
    avg_frequency = mean(claim_frequency),
    total_premium = sum(total_premium),
    n = n()
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = avg_frequency,
             y = avg_severity,
             size = total_premium,
             colour = policy_type,
             label = policy_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3,
                            box.padding = 0.5,
                            point.padding = 0.3) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(3, 15),
                        labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma,
                     expand = expansion(mult = 0.2)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma,
                     expand = expansion(mult = 0.2)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Comprehensive with Excess"    = "#4a7c6f",
    "Comprehensive without Excess" = "#7aadab",
    "Third-Party"                  = "#d4e8e1",
    "Third-Party Combined"         = "#2d5a51",
    "Third-Party with Glass"       = "#a8d5c2")) +
  labs(title = "Severity vs Frequency by Policy Type",
       subtitle = "Bubble size = total premium volume",
       x = "Average Claim Frequency",

```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-49-1.png){width=672}
:::

The bubble chart shows that Comprehensive without Excess policies have the highest claim frequency and highest severity but the premiums collected from this group are relatively low. This means the insurer is likely losing money on this segment.Third-Party Combined is the largest segment by premium volume, but policyholders claim very frequently. The saving grace is that individual claims tend to be cheaper. Comprehensive with Excess is the healthiest segment, claims are less frequent, and the large premium volume means the insurer is well-positioned to cover losses. ## 6.3 Factors Affecting Loss Ratio (Profitability)

`loss_ratio` is used as the measure of portfolio profitability. Only policies with a loss ratio between 0 and 10 are included to exclude extreme outliers that may distort the analysis.

`ggbetweenstats()` of the **ggstatsplot** package is used throughout this section as it integrates the visual comparison of group distributions with the results of a nonparametric statistical test in a single figure. The nonparametric Kruskal-Wallis test is chosen because `loss_ratio` is non-normally distributed and right-skewed, violating the normality assumption required for ANOVA.

::: panel-tabset
## Bonus Score

The violin plot below examines whether `bonus_score` — reflecting a policyholder's past claims history — has a significant effect on `loss_ratio`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = bonus_score,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Bonus Score",
  xlab = "Bonus Score",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  palette = "Set2"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-50-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio across bonus score groups (p = 2.07e-19). Interestingly, policyholders with a **Good** bonus score have the highest median loss ratio of **2.02**, followed by **Neutral** (1.60) and **Bad** (1.44). This counterintuitive finding may be attributed to the fact that Good bonus score policyholders dominate the portfolio (n = 35,663) and tend to hold more comprehensive policies, which attract higher claim costs. All pairwise comparisons are statistically significant, confirming that bonus score is a meaningful differentiator of portfolio profitability.

# 7 Summary

This study applied univariate and bivariate visual analytics techniques to explore a real-world motor vehicle insurance portfolio from a Spanish non-life insurance company, with the aim of uncovering key factors influencing portfolio profitability and volatility.

## Key Findings

**Portfolio Overview**

The portfolio is dominated by middle-aged drivers holding Third-Party Combined policies, with the vast majority of policyholders recording no claims. This suggests a generally low-risk customer base, with a small but significant minority of high-risk policyholders driving most of the portfolio's losses.

**Profitability (Loss Ratio)**

Policy type emerges as the strongest differentiator of portfolio profitability. Basic Third-Party policies are the least profitable despite being the least common, while Comprehensive with Excess policies generate the most consistent and stable loss ratios. This suggests that broader coverage does not necessarily translate to higher losses — comprehensive policyholders tend to be more careful drivers who pay higher premiums.

Fuel type and circulation area, while statistically significant, have negligible practical impact on profitability and are unlikely to be meaningful underwriting factors on their own.

**Volatility (Claim Frequency)**

Bonus score is the most powerful predictor of claim frequency. Policyholders with a Bad bonus score make significantly more frequent claims, contributing disproportionately to portfolio volatility. This confirms that past claims history is a reliable indicator of future claim behaviour.

Comprehensive without Excess policies stand out as the most volatile policy type, likely because the absence of an excess removes the financial disincentive to file small claims.

## Recommendations for Future Research

-   **Confirmatory Data Analysis** — Future studies should incorporate regression modelling to validate the findings from this exploratory analysis and establish more definitive relationships between the identified risk factors and portfolio outcomes.

-   **Exploring Additional Variables** — Variables such as driver occupation, annual mileage, claims history duration and vehicle usage type could provide a more complete picture of risk segmentation.

-   **Longitudinal Analysis** — Conducting longitudinal studies to track changes in loss ratios and claim frequencies over a longer time period can reveal trends and seasonal patterns that a three-year snapshot may not capture.

-   **Incorporating External Data** — As highlighted by the Swiss Re Institute (2019), enriching the portfolio data with external sources such as traffic density, weather patterns and regional accident statistics could further refine segmentation and improve underwriting strategy.
